coredata.dc:title: Valorization of cellulose pulp derived carbon quantum dots by controllable fractionation
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Wei
Jiang, Chuang
Zhang, Liguo
Li, Xiaoyu
Hou, Qingxi
Ni, Yonghao
coredata.dc:description: Fabrication and valorization of carbon quantum dots from lignocellulosic biomass is of significant importance. Herein, this study presents a simple and controllable route to prepare nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped CQDs (N,P-CQDs) in a one-pot synthesis from cellulose pulp. The subsequent fractionation based on molecular weight yielded three fractions of N,P-CQDs, i.e., N,P-CQDs-3500, N,P-CQDs-1000, and N,P-CQDs-500. The characteristics of each fraction N,P-CQDs were comprehensively evaluated. The fractionation endowed each fraction of N,P-CQDs with unique and different properties. The results showed that the resultant of N,P-CQDs were explored as a probe for pH detection, metal ions detection, and cell imaging. N,P-CQDs-500 exhibited unique selectivity towards Fe(II), which is novel and of importance in the biological process. The N,P-CQDs-1000 is sensitive to pH and has the potential to be a pH probe. The N,P-CQDs-3500 showed excellent bio-imaging ability in five cancer cells, and different performance on suspension and adherent cells. This study will not only help to expand the applications of lignocellulosic biomass for preparing CQDs, but also help to achieve upgrading of cellulose pulp into a high value-added product.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cellulose pulp
Carbon quantum dots
Fractionation
Fe(II) ions
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: pH stability and sensitivity of N,P-CQDs fractions
Sensitivity of N,P-CQDs fractions towards metal ions
Cell imaging ability of N,P-CQDs fractions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Science Foundation for Post-doctoral Scientists of China
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 32071715 ), and National Science Foundation for Post-doctoral Scientists of China (Grant No. 2019M651050 ). The authors appreciate the great technical support for the TEM observation from Tsinghua University, China, and support for the cell imaging from Peking Union Medical College Hospital, China.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 32071715), and National Science Foundation for Post-doctoral Scientists of China (Grant No. 2019M651050). The authors appreciate the great technical support for the TEM observation from Tsinghua University, China, and support for the cell imaging from Peking Union Medical College Hospital, China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of fabrication and fractionation of N,P-CQDs.

FTIR (a) and XPS spectra (b) of N,P-CQDs; Fluorescence decay of N,P-CQDs-500, N,P-CQDs-1000, and N,P-CQDs-3500 as a function of time at λex/λem of 340/430 nm/nm (c); UV-Vis absorption spectra, fluorescence spectra and UV-Vis photos (d), and 3D fluorescence spectra (e) of N,P-CQDs-500 (1), N,P-CQDs-1000 (2), and N,P -CQDs-3500 (3), respectively.

Effect of pH on photoluminescent of N,P-CQDs-500 (a), N,P-CQDs-1000 (b), N,P-CQDs-3500 (c), the relationship between fluorescence intensity and pH of N,P-CQDs-500 (d), N,P-CQDs-1000 (e), N,P-CQDs-3500 (f).

Fluorescence intensity of N,P-CQDs in presence of various metal ions (a); Fluorescence intensity of N,P-CQDs in presence of Fe(III), Fe(II) and Ag(I) (b); Fluorescence of the films containing N,P-CQDs-3500and N,P-CQDs-500 (the texts were written using Fe(III) and Fe(II) aqueous solution, respectively) at 365 nm UV light (c); The quenching mechanism of Fe(III) on N,P-CQDs-1000 and N,P-CQDs-3500 (d1), and quenching mechanism of Fe(III) and Fe(II) ions on N,P-CQDs-500 (d2).

Fluorescence intensity of N,P-CQDs-500 after adding different concentrations of Fe(II) ions (a); fluorescence intensity of N,P-CQDs-500 (b), N,P-CQDs-1000 (c) and N,P-CQDs-3500 (d) after adding different concentrations of Fe (III) ions.

Confocal fluorescence images of H1650, HEPG, HelaS3, Raji, and RPMI8226 cells incubated without (a, b, c, d, e) and with addition of N,P-CQDs-3500 (a1, b1, c1, d1, e1) under excitation wavelength of 405 nm, 488 nm, and bright field (DIC), respectively.
Elements of BSKP and N,P-CQDs (in atomic %).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: a, results from elemental analysis; b, results from XPS analysis. ND, not detected.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Regression Equations, Linear Range, and Detection Limit of N,P-CQDs for Fe(II) and Fe(III) Ions.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: The detection limit was obtained based on the signal-to-noise ratio of 3.0.

head.ce:title: Valorization of cellulose pulp derived carbon quantum dots by controllable fractionation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Pulp & Paper, Tianjin University of Science & Technology, Tianjin 300457, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Pulp & Paper, Tianjin University of Science & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, University of New Brunswick, Fredericton, New Brunswick, E3B 5A3, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering, University of New Brunswick, Fredericton

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, University of New Brunswick, Fredericton, New Brunswick, E3B 5A3, Canada

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Pulp & Paper, Tianjin University of Science & Technology, Tianjin 300457, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Pulp & Paper, Tianjin University of Science & Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Liguo Zhang and Chuang Jiang contributed equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fabrication and valorization of carbon quantum dots from lignocellulosic biomass is of significant importance. Herein, this study presents a simple and controllable route to prepare nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped CQDs (N,P-CQDs) in a one-pot synthesis from cellulose pulp. The subsequent fractionation based on molecular weight yielded three fractions of N,P-CQDs, i.e., N,P-CQDs-3500, N,P-CQDs-1000, and N,P-CQDs-500. The characteristics of each fraction N,P-CQDs were comprehensively evaluated. The fractionation endowed each fraction of N,P-CQDs with unique and different properties. The results showed that the resultant of N,P-CQDs were explored as a probe for pH detection, metal ions detection, and cell imaging. N,P-CQDs-500 exhibited unique selectivity towards Fe(II), which is novel and of importance in the biological process. The N,P-CQDs-1000 is sensitive to pH and has the potential to be a pH probe. The N,P-CQDs-3500 showed excellent bio-imaging ability in five cancer cells, and different performance on suspension and adherent cells. This study will not only help to expand the applications of lignocellulosic biomass for preparing CQDs, but also help to achieve upgrading of cellulose pulp into a high value-added product.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based nanomaterials as emerging materials have been attracting more and more research interests. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as one of typical carbon-based nanomaterials exhibit distinguished advantages such as environmental friendliness, good photoluminescence, biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and special recognition for metal ions (Tian et al., 2021). Thus, CQDs present a good potential prospect in bio-imaging, fluorescent probes, fluorescent inks, electrochemical sensors (Li et al., 2019), photocatalysis, and heavy metal detection, as well as drug delivery (Semeniuk et al., 2019).

CQDs can be produced from macroscopic carbon materials such as coal and green pepper (Das et al., 2019), and some carbon precursors with small organic molecules, e.g., glucose (Chen et al., 2018), citric acid (Zhang et al., 2018), and oxalic acid (Yang et al., 2019). Lignocellulosic biomass has been gaining much attention since it is abundant, environmentally-friendly, renewable, and biocompatibility (Huang et al., 2020), whose high value-added utilization has already become an urgent topic. Cellulose pulp is one of typical and versatile bio-products that is manufactured from renewable lignocellulosic biomass such as wood and non-wood. In 2021, the pulp production in the world and China is 180.93 and 81.77 million tons, respectively. Due to high carbon content, relatively pure chemical components including carbohydrates and lignin, and easy modifications via introducing some new functional groups, it is expected to be one potential good precursor for CQDs production. CQDs are a class of heterogeneous zero-dimensional spherical nanoparticles within the range of 1–10 nm (Pang et al., 2022). The heterogeneity of CQDs is mainly attributed to precursors and fabrication methods, that is, 1) the precursors especially these natural materials lack homogeneity; 2) most preparation methods are versatile and cannot be well controlled (Zhou et al., 2018), e.g., many reaction pathways occur during hydrothermal reaction of biomass to generate CQDs. Thus, a mixture with a wide range of particle size distribution and different surface functional moieties are obtained, endowing CQDs with varied and unique optical properties (Yang et al., 2021). For example, Jiang et al. (2015) found that the carbon dots with different particle sizes and nitrogen contents had different photoluminescent and up-conversion photoluminescent performance. It is expected that the fractionation cannot only diversify the products but also would upgrade the resultant CQDs. Therefore, it is very necessary to fractionate CQDs into different particle sizes. So far, some methods for separating CQDs have been developed, such as membrane separation, gel electrophoresis, capillary electrophoresis, and high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Vinci and Colon, 2012; Zhou et al., 2018). However, some disadvantages, e.g., low separation efficiency of gel electrophoresis, low separation yield of capillary electrophoresis, and high separation cost of HPLC, severely limit their applications in CQDs separation.

Iron, an essential and most abundant trace elements in human (3.0 −4.0 g in adults on average) (Van den Bosch et al., 2015), is significant in living biological system (Gudmundsson et al., 2021). Both deficiency and overload of iron can cause human disease, e.g., anemia and cardiovascular disease, osteoblast apoptosis, osteoporosis, kidney disease, and cancer, as well as Parkinson’s and Alzheimer’s diseases (Feng et al., 2021; Raha et al., 2022; Raichoudhury and Spinowitz, 2021). In human body, the irons mainly exist in the form of Fe(II) ions instead of Fe(III) ions (Gong et al., 2019). Fe(II) ions are the key to oxygen transport and storage, and are also crucial in energy metabolism. Thus, determination of iron content especially Fe(II) ions in human is very important. Some traditional methods for detecting iron ions, e.g., spectrophotometry, atomic absorption spectroscopy, and voltammetry (Peng et al., 2015; Sadak et al., 2017), need complex pretreatment and operation of samples, and expensive instrument (Gao et al., 2019). Thanks to high sensitivity, high selectivity, simple operation, and require no additional pretreatment for the sample testing, fluorescence probes made from CQDs have been used in detecting iron ions. However, most fluorescent sensors based on CQDs have been used to directly detect Fe(III) ions rather than Fe(II) ions (Architha et al., 2021). To our best knowledge, few reports on detecting Fe(II) ions are found, but they are conducted by oxidation of Fe(II) into Fe(III) with the assist of hydrogen peroxide (Wang et al., 2018). This places severe restrictions on the practical applications of CQDs-based fluorescent probes.

In this study, CQDs (N,P-CQDs) were fabricated from bleached softwood kraft pulp (BSKP) using one-pot hydrothermal method. Then, three fractions of N,P-CQDs were controllably fabricated via gradual fractionation using dialysis tubes with different molecular weights (i.e., N,P-CQDs-3500, N,P-CQDs-1000, and N,P-CQDs-500). The chemical and optical properties of each fraction N,P-CQDs were comprehensively evaluated and characterized. Then, three fractions of N,P-CQDs were explored as a probe for pH detection, metal ions detection, and cell imaging. To the best of our knowledge, this is initially to controllably fractionate the cellulose pulp-derived nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped CQDs into different types of N,P-CQDs, endowing each fraction with unique and different properties; N,P-CQDs-500 showed distinguished fluorescence quenching effect on both Fe(II) and Fe(III) ions, filling the current gap that CQDs cannot well detect Fe(II) ions; the as-prepared N,P-CQDs-3500 well recognized adherent and suspension cells. Targeted application of N,P-CQDs at different levels will help to enhance its application value, so as to maximize the utilization of lignocellulose biomass resources as far as possible.

The bleached softwood kraft pulp (BSKP) was kindly provided by a paper mill in Shandong Province, China. The BSKP was soaked in deionized water at room temperature for 4 h, and torn into pieces about 25 × 25 mm. The pulp was then disintegrated thoroughly in a disintegrator for 15,000 revolutions at a consistency of 15% to ensure that the pulp fibers were completely dispersed. The chemical compositions of BSKP were measured to be: 79.22% glucan, 14.72% xylan, 0.97% acid-insoluble lignin, 0.46% acid-soluble lignin, 3.27% ethanol extractives, and 0.32% ash (based on oven-dry weight), which was conducted according to NREL/TP-510–42618 and NREL/TP-510–42619.

Ammonium phosphate dibasic ((NH4)2HPO4) was obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., China. KCl, ZnCl2, Ni(CH3COO)2, and Fe2(SO4)3 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. CdCl2, CuSO4, Al(NO3)3, and AgNO3 were purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute, China. MnSO4 was obtained from Tianjin Jiangtian Chemical Technology Co., Ltd., China. All chemicals used in this study were of analytical grade. The cellulose nanofibrils (CNF) were obtained from Tianjin Woodelf Biotechnology Co., Ltd., China.

BSKP (12.0 g) and (NH4)2HPO4 (12.0 g) were thoroughly stirred at room temperature, transferred to a stainless steel autoclave (Model 4566, Parr Instrument Company, USA), and hydrothermally treated at 180 °C for 5 h with a stirring speed of 200 rpm. Once the hydrothermal treatment was complete, the suspension was filtered using a 0.22 µm Solarbio cylindrical filtration membrane filter. Finally, the obtained filtrate were collected, i.e., nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped CQDs (N,P-CQDs), then N,P-CQDs were further fractionated via dialysis ( Fig. 1). Firstly, the filtrate was dialyzed in deionized water using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight (Mw) cutoff of 3500 Da at room temperature for 2 days. During the period, deionized water was changed every 6 h. The part remaining in this dialysis tube was collected, concentrated in a rotary evaporator, and dried by lyophilization, i.e., N,P-CQDs-3500. Secondly, the liquid that passed through the dialysis tube with an Mw cutoff of 3500 Da was sequentially dialyzed using a dialysis tube with an Mw cutoff of 1000 Da, and designated as N,P-CQDs-1000. Thirdly, the liquid passed through this dialysis tube was sequentially dialyzed using a dialysis tube with an Mw cutoff of 500 Da, and designated as N,P-CQDs-500. Finally, N,P-CQDs-3500, N,P-CQDs-1000, and N,P-CQDs-500 were stored at about 4 °C for later use.

The as-prepared N,P-CQDs were comprehensively characterized using various analytical techniques. FTIR spectra of test samples were recorded using a spectrophotometer (650, Tianjin Gangdong Sci. &Tech. Development Co., Ltd., China) in the range of 4000 − 400 cm−1, and scanned 32 times at a resolution of 4 cm−1. UV-Vis absorption spectra of test samples were obtained on a spectrophotometer (DR-6000, HACH, USA). Fluorescence spectra of test samples were obtained using a fluorescence spectrometer (F-7100, Hitachi Co., Ltd., Japan). The surface functional groups were measured with XPS using an Escalab 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fischer, USA) with a mono X-Ray source Al Kα excitation (1350.08 eV). The elemental analysis of BSKP was carried out in an organic element analyzer (Elementar vario EL cube, Elementar, USA) and coupled with plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) (ICAP Q, Thermo Scientific, USA).

TEM and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of test samples were observed by a TEM (Tecnai G2 F20, FEI, USA) operated at 200 kV. Before the observation, the as-prepared N,P-CQDs were dispersed in 95% (v/v) ethanol and treated with ultrasound for 30 min. One drop of the solution was deposited onto a carbon-coated copper TEM grid (200 mesh). The morphology and microstructure of test samples were observed by using a SEM (JSM-IT300LV, JEOL Co., Ltd., Japan). Combined with the SEM analysis, the elemental analysis was conducted using energy disperse spectroscopy (EDS, X-MaxN20, Oxford Instruments Group, UK). The time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy of test samples was recorded by a steady state transient fluorescence spectrometer (FLS1000, Edinburgh, UK).

Buffer solutions with different pH values in the range of 2.0–12.0 were prepared using hydrochloric acid (10 mmol L−1), sodium citrate buffer (50 mmol L−1), phosphate buffer (50 mmol L−1), sodium bicarbonate-sodium hydroxide buffer (50 mmol L−1), and sodium hydroxide buffer (10 mmol L−1), respectively. N,P-CQDs solution (1.0 mg mL−1, 100 μL) and 1.0 mL of the buffer solution with different pH values were uniformly mixed, respectively. To evaluate the stability of the resultant N,P-CQDs under different pH values, the fluorescence intensity for these above solutions were performed with fluorescence spectrometer.

Fe2(SO4)3 (3.0 g) was dissolved in deionized water and prepared into Fe(III) ionic solutions of different concentrations. N,P-CQDs aqueous solution, Fe(III) ionic solution and citrate - sodium citrate buffer at pH 3.0 were mixed. After stabilization, the emission wavelength at the optimal excitation wavelength was determined by fluorescence spectrometer (F-7100, Hitachi Co., Ltd., Japan). FeCl2 was dissolved in deionized water and configured into Fe(II) ionic solutions of different concentrations. N,P-CQDs-500 aqueous solution, Fe(II) ionic solution and phosphate buffer with pH 6.0 were mixed. After stabilization, the emission wavelength at the optimal excitation wavelength was determined by fluorescence spectrometer (F-7100, Hitachi Co., Ltd., Japan).

Nine kinds of salt solutions including KCl, ZnCl2, CuSO4, Al(NO3)3, AgNO3, CdCl2, Ni(CH3COO)2, Fe2(SO4)3, and MnSO4 with a concentration of 10 mmol L−1 were prepared with deionized water, respectively. A 0.5 mL N,P-CQDs aqueous solution (1 mg mL−1) and 0.5 mL various salt solutions were then evenly mixed in a 2.0 mL test tube, respectively, the mixed solution was equilibrated for 5 min before measurement. The fluorescence spectra were obtained at the excitation wavelength of 318 nm.

The linear relationship between F
0
/F and the concentration of Fe(III) or Fe(II) ions was well fitted to Stern-Volmer equation (Wang et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2019).
(1)
F
0
F
=
K
SV
C + 1
where K sv is the quenching constant, C is the concentration of N,P-CQDs, F and F 0 are fluorescence intensity of N,P-CQDs before and after the addition of iron ions, respectively.

Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, 20%, 2.0 mL) was added into CNF (1.0 wt%, 18 mL) under vigorous stirring 30 min. Meanwhile, N,P-CQDs-3500 (1.0 mL) was added into the mixture, and kept ultrasonic treatment for 30 min. The mixture was added into plastic petri dish with a diameter of 8 cm for a period to allow the solvent evaporation in ambient temperature. After that, the mixture will self-assemble into solid film, named CNF/N,P-CQDs-3500. The film of CNF/N,P-CQDs-500 was also prepared by incorporation of N,P-CQDs-500 in CNF via the same process.

Five kinds of cancer cells including H1650, HEPG, HelaS3, Raji, and RPMI8226 cells were maintained in Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) (10% newborn bovine serum) and incubated at 37 °C in 5% CO2 for 24 h, respectively. Then, the N,P-CQDs (1.0 mg mL−1, 30 μL) were added into DMEM, and further incubated at 37 °C in 5% CO2 for 24 h. Finally, the cells were washed with PBS buffer solution three times to remove free N,P-CQDs, and observed under confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) (FV1000, Olympus Japan). The fluorescence signals were recorded by using 405 and 488 nm laser excitation, respectively.

Images of the three monodispersed N,P-CQDs were obtained by TEM (Fig. 1). The HRTEM images clearly demonstrated that the diffraction contrast of N,P-CQDs was getting weaker, i.e., the diffraction contrast of N,P-CQDs-3500 was the highest while that of both N,P-CQDs-1000 and N,P-CQDs-500 was very low. This result indicated that the thickness of three N,P-CQDs was gradually decreased in the order of N,P-CQDs-3500, N,P-CQDs-1000, and N,P-CQDs-500. No obvious lattice for the three N,P-CQDs, indicating a polymeric amorphous structure (Tang et al., 2017).

The functional groups of N,P-CQDs were characterized with the combination of FTIR and XPS. The surface functional groups of three N,P-CQDs were nearly the same ( Fig. 2a). The broad absorption peak at 3130 cm−1 resulted from the stretching vibration bands of N–H. The stretching vibration of CO was found at 1629 cm−1 (Shan et al., 2018). The characteristic peaks at 1080 cm−1 and 906 cm−1 were attributed to P–O–R (R=alkyl) and P–O–R (R=aromatic structure) groups, respectively (Wang et al., 2013). However, the absorption peaks at 3429 cm−1 and 1298 cm−1 were only found in N,P-CQDs-3500, which were ascribed to the stretching vibration of O–H and PO, respectively. Meanwhile, the peak at 1125 cm−1 in N,P-CQDs-500 can be correlated to CS bonds (Wei et al., 2018). The XPS spectra of the three N,P-CQDs showed binding energy peaks at 532.4, 400.9, 286.2, 190.8, and 135.6 eV, which were attributed to O1s, N1s, C1s, P2s, and P2p, respectively (Fig. 2b). This result confirmed that N and P were successfully introduced into the surface of N,P-CQDs. It’s worth noting that the peaks of N,P-CQDs-500 at 231.7 and 167.6 eV were resulted from S2s and S2p, respectively. As shown in Fig. S1, S2, and S3, the C1s XPS spectra of the N,P-CQDs-3500, N,P-CQDs-1000, and N,P-CQDs-500 showed the same peaks at 288.9, 286.3, and 284.9 eV, which are ascribed to the O–CO, C–P/C–N, and C–C/CC groups, respectively (Jing et al., 2019). However, the peak of C1s for N,P-CQDs-3500 and N,P-CQDs-1000 showed the contribution at 287.6 eV, which is ascribed to CO group (Zhang et al., 2019). The XPS spectra of N1s of three N,P-CQDs showed the same peaks at 401.2 and 399.7 eV, assigning to N–N/P–N and N–H groups, respectively. The peak at 402.0 eV of N,P-CQDs-3500 can be assigned to NO group, while the peak at 399.3 eV of N,P-CQDs-500 resulted from C–N–C group. The P2p spectra of N,P-CQDs-3500 and N,P-CQDs-500 showed two peaks at 134.0 and 133.3 eV, representing PO and P–C/P–N bonds, respectively (Qiao et al., 2018); the peaks of N,P-CQDs-1000 at 134.4 and 133.4 eV represented C/C2–P and P–C/P–N, respectively (Du et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2018b). The peaks of N,P-CQDs-500 were detected at 169.4 and 168.2 eV, which can be assigned to SO4 2- and R–SO3H (Travlou et al., 2018) (Fig. S3e). In addition, three N,P-CQDs showed similar fluorescence lifetime about 8.86 ns (Fig. 2c).

The elemental analysis results of the BSKP and those of N,P-CQDs are shown in Table 1. The results showed that the BSKP was mainly composed of elements C and O, and a small amount of elements N, P, and S. The contents of N and P in N,P-CQDs were much higher than those of BSKP. The results were consistent with the FTIR and XPS analysis. The SEM-EDS analysis also showed that both N (green dot) and P (yellow dot) were successfully doped into CQDs (Fig. S4a, b). The proportion of each element of three N,P-CQDs showed great difference, illustrating their different energy level structures, which can be applied for predicting fluorescence performance. The existence of element S was attributed to the sulfonate groups of BSKP, which was consistent with the elemental analysis of BSKP. The content of sulfur (S) (purple dot) in N,P-CQDs-500 was the highest among three N,P-CQDs (Fig. S4c), which is consistent with the XPS results. It is worth pointing out that S in N,P-CQDs-500 would complicate the surface structure.

The modification and passivation of surface functional groups of CQDs by heteroatoms doping can improve the optical properties. Nitrogen has five valence electrons that can bond with carbon atom to form n-type semiconductor (Yang et al., 2018a). Phosphorus an n-type donor can form a substitution defect in sp3-bonded diamond thin films (Shan et al., 2018). Hence, it is believed that co-doping elements of N and P can help to improve the potential active sites of CQDs, accelerate the transfer of electrons, and enrich the surface functional groups, thus improving the detection ability of metal ions and other unanticipated properties of CQDs. The results showed that all the N,P-CQDs had good fluorescence.

The UV-Vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of the N,P-CQDs were evaluated. The UV-Vis absorption spectra showed typical characteristic absorption peaks in the range of 200–400 nm (labeled by red shades), i.e., the absorption shoulder peaks at around 220 nm and 260 nm. Comparing the absorption spectra of the three types of N,P-CQDs, it was found that the absorption peaks of N,P-CQDs-3500 and N,P-CQDs-1000 were extended to the visible region, which is expected to provide a great potential for bioimaging. The maximum excitation/emission wavelengths of N,P-CQDs-3500, N,P-CQDs-1000, and N,P-CQDs-500 were 330/412, 330/436, and 340/438 nm/nm, respectively. The three N,P-CQDs showed similar photoluminescence properties (Fig. 2d and Fig.S5); the emission wavelength appeared to shift to the red as the excitation wavelength increased from 300 to 400 nm. 3D fluorescence spectra of N,P-CQDs-500, N,P-CQDs-1000, and N,P-CQDs-3500 exhibited good fluorescence properties, with different fluorescence performance and emission wavelengths, in which N,P-CQDs-3500 showed the strongest fluorescence intensity (Fig. 2e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH stability and sensitivity of N,P-CQDs fractions
ce:section.ce:para: To further understand the stability and explore N,P-CQDs as fluorescent probes, the influence of pH on the fluorescence intensity of N,P-CQDs was studied ( Fig. 3). The fluorescence intensity of N,P-CQDs-1000 fluctuated greatly with pH value, and its fluorescence intensity was much stronger when pH value was 3.0 than that under other pH values (Fig. 3b), indicating its high sensitivity to strong acid. The fluorescence intensity of N,P-CQDs-500 (Fig. 3a) and N,P-CQDs-3500 (Fig. 3c) did not show significant changes, indicating that N,P-CQDs-500 and N,P-CQDs-3500 were relatively stable under both strongly acidic and weakly alkaline environments. The fluorescence intensity of three types of N,P-CQDs were linear with the pH over the range of 6–12 (Fig. 3d-f). It is worth nothing that N,P-CQDs-1000 showed very good linear with pH of 7–12. This indicates that the pH especially in some special environments the can be obtained via measuring fluorescence intensity of N,P-CQDs. So, the pH-sensitive N,P-CQDs-1000 could be used to monitor reactions that may lead to a minor change in pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of N,P-CQDs fractions towards metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: N,P-CQDs showed different responses to metal ions including Ag(I), Zn(II), Ni(II), Cd(II), K(I), Al(III), Fe(III), Cu(II), Fe(II), and Mn(II) ( Fig. 4a). The varied sensitivity of N, P-CQDs to different metal ions can be used to identify and measure metal ions. The results showed that 1) among these metal ions, Fe(III), Fe(II), and Ag(I) showed obvious fluorescence quenching effect on N,P-CQDs, in which Fe(III) ions showed the most obvious fluorescence quenching effect; 2) with the decrease of molecular weight, the sensitivities of N,P-CQDs fractionations towards Fe(III), Fe(II) and Ag(I) became higher, indicating that the fractionation enhanced the sensitivity of N,P-CQDs; 3) in addition, compared with N,P-CQDs-3500 and N,P-CQDs-1000, N,P-CQDs-500 showed better sensitivity to Fe(II) (Fig. 4b). The fluorescence of the films prepared using CNF and N,P-CQDs-3500 or N,P-CQDs-500 (Fig. 4c) were observed at 365 nm UV light. When Fe(III) ink was written on the film with N,P-CQDs-3500, the blue fluorescence disappeared. Similarly, the word of Fe(II) presented on the film that contains N,P-CQDs-500 after addition of Fe(II) ions. It was further demonstrated that both Fe(III) and Fe(II) ions had obvious fluorescence quenching effect on N,P-CQDs-3500 and N,P-CQDs-500, respectively. This could be potentially used in anti-counterfeiting and encryption aspects.

The hypothetical mechanism of N,P-CQDs fluorescence quenching by Fe(III) and Fe(II) ions was proposed and illustrated in Fig. 4d. The photoluminescence of N,P-CQDs was generated due to the electron transition between highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) under the exposure to ultraviolet radiation. When the iron ions were added into N,P-CQDs solutions, the quenching effects were caused by the chelation of iron ions and surface functional groups (i.e., phenolic hydroxyl, amino, and phosphate acid groups) or holes of N,P-CQDs. Then, the electrons in LUMO of N,P-CQDs transferred to 3d orbital of iron ions, the transfer of non-radiation electron occurred during this process (Semeniuk et al., 2019). It is worth pointing out that, due to the distinguished sensitivity to Fe(II) ions, N,P-CQDs-500 fill the current gap that CQDs cannot detect Fe(II) ions as fluorescent probes. As for N,P-CQDs-500, there are not only hydroxyl group, amino group, and phosphoric acid group, but also R–SO3H on the surface of N,P-CQDs-500. The R–SO3H groups may exist on edge or defect site of N,P-CQDs-500, which would provide the bridge between N,P-CQDs-500 and Fe(II) ions. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of Fe(II) ions by N,P-CQDs-500 was generated.

Based on the above analysis, we found that N,P-CQDs-500 had great potential applications in the detection of both Fe(II) and Fe(III) ions. The fluorescence quenching effect of N,P-CQDs-500 on both Fe(III) and Fe(II) ions were then evaluated in detail, as shown in Fig. 5a,b, respectively. The fluorescence intensities of the N,P-CQDs-500 were gradually reduced with increasing concentrations of both Fe(III) and Fe(II) ions. Similar phenomenon was found for both N,P-CQDs-1000 and N,P-CQDs-3500 under different concentrations of Fe(III) ions, respectively, as shown in Fig. 5c, d.

The regression equations between F
0
/F and the concentration of Fe(III) or Fe(II) ions were summarized in Table 2. The detection limits of N,P-CQDs-3500 and N,P-CQDs-1000 for Fe(III) ions were 1.380 and 1.670 μmol L−1, respectively, with the linear range of 0–0.452 mmol L−1. The detection limit of N,P-CQDs-500 for Fe(III) ions and Fe(II) ions were 1.278 and 3.070 μmol L−1, with the linear range of 0–0.362 and 1.356–4.972 mmol L−1, respectively. Compared with the detection limit of Fe (III) in the literatures, e.g., 1.5 μmol L−1 (Doan et al., 2022), 3.04 μmol L−1 (Qian et al., 2020), and the detection limit of Fe (II), e.g., 2.15 μmol L−1 (Zheng et al., 2020), 7.15 μmol L−1 (Kumar et al., 2021), the as-prepared N,P-CQDs showed a good detection limit. Moreover, the detection limits were much lower than that of iron ions in human blood (7.52–11.82 mmol L−1) (Zhang et al., 2019), which provides the application possibility of N,P-CQDs in biological medical field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell imaging ability of N,P-CQDs fractions
ce:section.ce:para: It is reported that the iron content in tumor cells is much higher than that in normal cells (Zhang et al., 2020). Therefore, N,P-CQDs would be potentially used for cell imaging and identifing cancer cells due to its unique sensibility to iron ions. Thanks to relatively good and stable fluorescence performance in the pH range of 5.0–7.0, absorption peaks extending to visible region, and the strongest fluorescence intensity among the three N,P-CQDs, N,P-CQDs-3500 was chosen and explored in cell imaging and photocatalysis. N,P-CQDs-3500 was introduced into two types of cancer cells: adherent cells (H1650, HEPG, and HelaS3) and suspension cells (Raji and RPMI8226), respectively. The fluorescence intensity of these cells were significantly increased before ( Fig. 6a-e) and after (Fig. 6a1-e1) the addition of 1.0 mg mL−1 N,P-CQDs-3500 and cultivation for 24 h. The survival of these cells after 24 h of cultivation demonstrates a good biocompatibility of N,P-CQDs-3500. The cells of H1650, HEPG, HelaS3, Raji, and RPMI8226 exhibited bright blue and green colors emission at the excitation wavelength of 405 nm and 488 nm, respectively, which was consistent with the previous report (Sharma et al., 2018). It can be clearly seen that N,P-CQDs-3500 showed good fluorescence in cells of H1650, HEPG, HelaS3, Raji, and RPMI8226 (Fig. 6a1-e1), suggesting that N,P-CQDs-3500 would be potentially applied for discriminating these cancer cells. It is worth pointing out that N,P-CQDs-3500 showed different performance on these two types of cells. That is, N,P-CQDs-3500 can adhere to the cell membrane of adherent cells (H1650, HEPG, and HelaS3), as shown in Fig. 6a1-c1. On the other hand, N,P-CQDs-3500 can enter the cytoplasm of suspension cells (Raji and RPMI8226) (Fig. 6d1-e1). It is very interesting that the N,P-CQDs-3500 differs significantly from traditional fluorescent dyes, i.e., a fixed fluorescent dye works on the same sites of different cells. It would provide a new approach for the identification and classification of unknown cells into suspension and adherent cells. Thus, in addition to cell imaging, this study would fill the gap in the current imaging of suspension cells with CQDs.

The fluorescence effect of N,P-CQDs-3500 on the suspension cells was much better than that of adherent cells since it can enter the cytoplasm of suspension cells. As depicted in Fig. 6d1-e1, there was no clear difference between these two suspension cells of Raji and RPMI8226, suggesting that N,P-CQDs-3500 can be applied in both types of suspension cells. By comparing the fluorescence performance of N,P-CQDs-3500 in H1650, HEPG, and HelaS3, we found that N,P-CQDs-3500 showed superior selectivity for H1650.

The N,P-CQDs were fabricated from cellulose pulp using one-pot hydrothermal method, and further controllably fractionated into three fractions with different molecular weights and properties. N and P co-doping of CQDs resulted in significant improvement in electronic properties and surface chemical activities, improving the optical properties of N,P-CQDs. The fractionation enhanced the sensitivity of N,P-CQDs towards metal ions including Fe(III), Fe(II) and Ag(I). The absorption peaks of N,P-CQDs-1000 and N,P-CQDs-3500 were extend to visible region. N,P-CQDs-500 and N,P-CQDs-3500 were stable within the pH range of 2.0–10.0 and 2.0–8.0, respectively, but the fluorescence intensity of N,P-CQDs-1000 varied with pH values. N,P-CQDs-3500 showed good quenching effect on Fe(III) ions, and good fluorescence performance in suspension cells (Raji and RPMI8226) and superior selectivity for adherent cells (H1650). N,P-CQDs-1000 and N,P-CQDs-500 showed enhanced quenching effect on Fe(II) ions in addition to Fe(III) ions. N,P-CQDs-500 was most sensitive to Fe(II) ions due to hydroxyl, amino, and phosphoric acid groups, especially –SOx on the surface of N,P-CQDs-500.

Wei Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Liguo Zhang: Investigation, Resources, Validation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Chuang Jiang: Investigation, Resources, Validation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Xiaoyu Li: Investigation, Resources, Visualization. Qingxi Hou: Methodology, Supervision. Yonghao Ni: Writing – original draft, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 32071715), and National Science Foundation for Post-doctoral Scientists of China (Grant No. 2019M651050). The authors appreciate the great technical support for the TEM observation from Tsinghua University, China, and support for the cell imaging from Peking Union Medical College Hospital, China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.indcrop.2022.115560.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
